questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Read the following write up carefully and answer the following questions: Let $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_3}, \ldots ., \overline{\mathrm{OP}_n}$ be unit vectors in a plane. $\mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \mathrm{P}_3, \ldots . \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}$ all lie on the same side of a line through $O .|\overline{\mathrm{OM}}|$ denotes length of vector through $O$Question: $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{v}}} \cdot\left(\overline{\mathrm{OP}_3}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_4}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_5}+\ldots . .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}-1}}\right)\left(\right.$ where $\left.\overline{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{r}}}=\overline{\mathrm{P}_2}+\overline{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $\overrightarrow{P_{\mathrm{v}}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ so $\overline{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{v}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{P}_2}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_3}, \ldots ., \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$
So, $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{v}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_3}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}-1}}\right)\gt0$ Again, if $n$ is odd $\Rightarrow n=2 k-1$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} $$ Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2} \ldots . . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}+1}}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}\right) \geq \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}=1 $$ Now, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right) \leq\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots . .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_n}\right|$ Again, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$ makes acute angle with all the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \ldots \ldots . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}$ So, $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}|=\left|\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots \ldots+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}\right| \geq 1 \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}| \geq 1$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph Three forces $\vec{f}_1, \vec{f}_2 \& \vec{f}_3$ of magnitude 2,4 and 6 units respectively act along three face diagonals of a cube as shown in figure. Let $\mathrm{P}_1$ be a parallelopiped whose three co-terminus edges be three vectors $\vec{f}_1, \vec{f}_2 \& \vec{f}_3$. Let the joning of mid-points of each pair of opposite edges of parallelopiped $P_1$ meet in point $X$. On the basis of above information, answer the following questions :
Question The magnitude of the resultant of the three forces is -
questions.solution: Let $O A, O C \& O G$ represents $x, y \& z$ axis respectively \& let $\ell(\mathrm{OA})=\ell(\mathrm{OC})=\ell(\mathrm{OG})=1$ $\vec{f}_1=2 \frac{(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\vec{f}_2=4 \frac{(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\vec{f}_3=6 \frac{(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}})}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\vec{f}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=\sqrt{2}(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

Let $\mathrm{P}_1$ be a parallelopiped whose three co-terminus edges be three vectors $\vec{f}_1, \vec{f}_2 \& \vec{f}_3$. Question $\ell(O X)$ is equal to -

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: A line through the origin has the vector equation $\bar{r}=\lambda \bar{s}, \lambda$ is a scalar parameter. Consider a family of line $L$ with $\overline{\mathrm{s}}=(\cos \theta+\sqrt{3}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\sqrt{2} \sin \theta) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\cos \theta-\sqrt{3}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ thenQuestion: The line through origin and making acute angle $\frac{\pi}{6}$ with family of lines of the L-family are in the form of
questions.solution:
Let $M$ (line be $\bar{r}=\mu(a i+b j+c k)$ then acute angle between $L$ and $M$ is


$\begin{aligned} & \cos \alpha=\frac{(a+c) \cos \theta+\sqrt{2} b \sin \theta+(a-c) \sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}} \\ & \alpha=\frac{\pi}{6}, a+c=0, b=0, c=-a \\ & \bar{M}=\mu(i-k)\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Read the following write up carefully and answer the following questions: Let $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_3}, \ldots ., \overline{\mathrm{OP}_n}$ be unit vectors in a plane. $\mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \mathrm{P}_3, \ldots . \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}$ all lie on the same side of a line through $O .|\overline{\mathrm{OM}}|$ denotes length of vector through $O$Question: If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_3}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_4}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_5}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_6}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_7}$ then least value of $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}|$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $\overrightarrow{P_{\mathrm{v}}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ so $\overline{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{v}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{P}_2}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_3}, \ldots ., \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$
So, $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{v}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_3}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}-1}}\right)\gt0$ Again, if $n$ is odd $\Rightarrow n=2 k-1$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} $$ Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2} \ldots . . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}+1}}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}\right) \geq \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}=1 $$ Now, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right) \leq\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots . .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_n}\right|$ Again, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$ makes acute angle with all the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \ldots \ldots . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}$ So, $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}|=\left|\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots \ldots+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}\right| \geq 1 \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}| \geq 1$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Read the following write up carefully and answer the following questions: Let $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_3}, \ldots ., \overline{\mathrm{OP}_n}$ be unit vectors in a plane. $\mathrm{P}_1, \mathrm{P}_2, \mathrm{P}_3, \ldots . \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}$ all lie on the same side of a line through $O .|\overline{\mathrm{OM}}|$ denotes length of vector through $O$Question: If n is odd then, $\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots . .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{n}}}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $\overrightarrow{P_{\mathrm{v}}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ so $\overline{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{v}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{P}_2}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_3}, \ldots ., \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{k}}}$
So, $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{v}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_3}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}-1}}\right)\gt0$ Again, if $n$ is odd $\Rightarrow n=2 k-1$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} $$ Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}$ will make acute angle with all the vectors from $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2} \ldots . . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}+1}}+\ldots .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{2 \mathrm{k}-1}}\right) \geq \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}=1 $$ Now, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}} \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right) \leq\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_{\mathrm{k}}}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\ldots .+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_{2 k-1}}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots . .+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}_n}\right|$ Again, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$ makes acute angle with all the vectors $\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}, \overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}, \ldots \ldots . \overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}$ So, $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}|=\left|\overline{\mathrm{OP}_1}+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_2}+\ldots \ldots+\overline{\mathrm{OP}_7}\right| \geq 1 \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}|+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{S}}| \geq 1$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: A line through the origin has the vector equation $\bar{r}=\lambda \bar{s}, \lambda$ is a scalar parameter. Consider a family of line $L$ with $\overline{\mathrm{s}}=(\cos \theta+\sqrt{3}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\sqrt{2} \sin \theta) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\cos \theta-\sqrt{3}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ thenQuestion: If the lines in $L$ meet the plane $x-z=4 \sqrt{3}$ at $P$. If $\theta$ varies and $P$ describes a circle on the plane with centre on $M$ then radius of this circle is
questions.solution:
$M$ meets the given plane in $P_1,(2 \sqrt{3}, 0,-2 \sqrt{3})$ line $L$ meets the plane in $\mathrm{P}(2 \cos \theta+2 \sqrt{3}, 2 \sqrt{2} \sin \theta, 2 \cos \theta-2 \sqrt{3})$
Thus $\left(\mathrm{PP}_1\right)^2=8$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}$ describes a circle of radius $2 \sqrt{2}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph Two vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ having unit modulus and angle between them is $\theta$. Now $\phi(\theta)=\int_{-(\vec{a} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^2}^{(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^2} f^2(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$ and $f$ satisfies the condition $f(\mathrm{y})+f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{xy}}$ for all $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{R}-\{0\}$ and $\mathrm{h}(\theta)=-\phi(\theta)+\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_1\right|^2\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_1\right|^2$, where $\left|b_1\right|=2|b|$. On the basis of above information, answer the following questions :Question The volume of the parallelopiped formed by $\vec{a}, \vec{b}$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$, where angle between $\vec{a}$ \& $\vec{b}$ is taken for which $h(\theta)$ is minimum is -
questions.solution:
$\phi(\theta)=\int_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta}(f(\mathrm{x}))^2 \mathrm{dx}$

Also $f(x)+f(y)=\frac{x+y}{x y}$
Put $y=x$
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \\
& \therefore \quad \phi(\theta)=\int_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta} \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\left[-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right]_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta}
\end{aligned}$

Now $\quad h(\theta)=-\phi(\theta)+\left|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}_1\right|^2\left|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}_1\right|^2$
$=4 \operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta+16 \sin ^2 \theta \cos ^2 \theta$
$=4\left(\operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta+\frac{1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta}\right)$
It will be minimum when $\operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta=1$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$
So volume of the parallelopiped will be
$[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})]=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^2=\frac{1}{2}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph Two vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ having unit modulus and angle between them is $\theta$. Now $\phi(\theta)=\int_{-(\vec{a} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^2}^{(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^2} f^2(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$ and $f$ satisfies the condition $f(\mathrm{y})+f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{xy}}$ for all $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{R}-\{0\}$ and $\mathrm{h}(\theta)=-\phi(\theta)+\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_1\right|^2\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_1\right|^2$, where $\left|b_1\right|=2|b|$. On the basis of above information, answer the following questions :Question If $\vec{a} \& \vec{b}$ are non collinear vectors, then number of solution of the equation $4 \phi(\theta)+\sqrt{3} \phi^{\prime}(\theta)=0$ in $[0$, $2 \pi]$, are -
questions.solution:
$\phi(\theta)=\int_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta}(f(\mathrm{x}))^2 \mathrm{dx}$

Also $f(x)+f(y)=\frac{x+y}{x y}$
Put $y=x$
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \\
& \therefore \quad \phi(\theta)=\int_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta} \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\left[-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right]_{-\cos ^2 \theta}^{\sin ^2 \theta}
\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & 4 \phi(\theta)+\sqrt{3} \phi^{\prime}(\theta)=0 \\ & -16 \operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta+16 \sqrt{3} \operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta \cot 2 \theta=0 \\ & 16 \operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta(-1+\sqrt{3} \cot 2 \theta)=0\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cot 2 \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \quad\left(\right.$ as $\left.\operatorname{cosec}^2 2 \theta \neq 0\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \tan 2 \theta=\sqrt{3}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \theta=\mathrm{n} \pi+\frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{6}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}$
So number of solutions in $[0,2 \pi]$ will be 4 .
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Consider the three vectors $\vec{p}, \vec{q}$ and $\vec{r}$ such that $\vec{p}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{q}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{p} \times \vec{r}=\vec{q}+c \vec{p}$ and $\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}=2$Question: The value of $[\vec{p} \vec{q} \vec{r}]$ is
questions.solution:
(i) Given $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \text { and } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=2
\end{aligned}$
cross with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ dot with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \text { and } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{0} \quad 0=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})$
$\therefore \quad(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(i)}$
$c=-\frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{p}}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(ii)}$
But $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}|=\mathrm{p}^2=3$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(iii)}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=1-1+1=1 \ldots$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(iv)}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -1 & 1
\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \ldots$ 
 $\qquad...\mathrm{(v)}$
Using (iii), (iv), (v) in (i) and (ii), we get
$3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-2 \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{k} \text { and } \mathrm{c}=-\frac{1}{3}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(vii)}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{3}[2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}]$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{3}(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(vi)}$
Now, $[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{4}{3}\end{array}\right|=\left(\frac{-4}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\right)-1\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)+1\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)$
$\therefore \quad[\overline{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{q} \mathrm{p}]=-2-\frac{2}{5}=-\frac{8}{3}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(viii)}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (ii) }[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}] \mathrm{x}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(-\frac{8}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \text { or }\left(-\frac{8}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-\frac{2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}}{3}\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{3} \text { Verify your self }\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{3}(3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})=-\frac{1}{4}(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=-\frac{1}{4}(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}-4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\end{aligned}$
(iii) As $\mathrm{c}=-\frac{1}{3}$ from (vii)
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \therefore \quad \frac{2}{3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(1) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}(\text { As } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=1 \text { from } \ldots . . \text { (iv)) }
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{3}{2}\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{4}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\frac{3}{2} \frac{(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{3} \\
& \vec{y}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+\hat{k} \\
& \therefore \quad[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-\frac{1}{2} & 1 & -\frac{1}{2} \\
3 & -4 & \frac{1}{3} \\
0 & \frac{2}{3} & \frac{4}{3}
\end{array}\right|=-\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{16}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\right)-1(4)-\frac{1}{2}(2) \\
& \therefore \quad[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=3-4-1=-2 \\
& \therefore \left\lvert\, \frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array} { l l l } 
{ \mathrm { x } } & { \vec { y } } & { \vec { \mathrm { r } } ] }
\end{array} \left|=\left|-\frac{1}{3}\right|=|\mathrm{c}|\right.\right.\right.
\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ are the coterminus edges of a tetrahedron whose volume is $|\mathrm{c}|$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Consider the three vectors $\vec{p}, \vec{q}$ and $\vec{r}$ such that $\vec{p}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{q}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k} ; \vec{p} \times \vec{r}=\vec{q}+c \vec{p}$ and $\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}=2$Question: If $\vec{x}$ is a vector such that $[\vec{p} \vec{q} \vec{r}] \vec{x}=(\vec{p} \times \vec{q}) \times \vec{r}$, then $\vec{x}$ is
questions.solution:
(i) Given $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \text { and } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=2
\end{aligned}$
cross with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$ dot with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \text { and } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c} \overrightarrow{0} \quad 0=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{c}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})$
$\therefore \quad(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(i)}$
$c=-\frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{p}}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(ii)}$
But $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}|=\mathrm{p}^2=3$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(iii)}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=1-1+1=1 \ldots$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(iv)}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -1 & 1
\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \ldots$ 
 $\qquad...\mathrm{(v)}$
Using (iii), (iv), (v) in (i) and (ii), we get
$3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}-2 \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{k} \text { and } \mathrm{c}=-\frac{1}{3}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(vii)}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{3}[2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}]$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{3}(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(vi)}$
Now, $[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{4}{3}\end{array}\right|=\left(\frac{-4}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\right)-1\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)+1\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)$
$\therefore \quad[\overline{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{q} \mathrm{p}]=-2-\frac{2}{5}=-\frac{8}{3}$ $\qquad...\mathrm{(viii)}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (ii) }[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}] \mathrm{x}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(-\frac{8}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \text { or }\left(-\frac{8}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-\frac{2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}}{3}\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2}{3} \text { Verify your self }\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{3}(3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})=-\frac{1}{4}(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=-\frac{1}{4}(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{i}}-4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{j}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\end{aligned}$
(iii) As $\mathrm{c}=-\frac{1}{3}$ from (vii)
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left(1-\frac{1}{3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \therefore \quad \frac{2}{3} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-(1) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}(\text { As } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=1 \text { from } \ldots . . \text { (iv)) }
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{3}{2}\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}-\frac{2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{4}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)=\frac{3}{2} \frac{(6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{3} \\
& \vec{y}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+\hat{k} \\
& \therefore \quad[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-\frac{1}{2} & 1 & -\frac{1}{2} \\
3 & -4 & \frac{1}{3} \\
0 & \frac{2}{3} & \frac{4}{3}
\end{array}\right|=-\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{16}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\right)-1(4)-\frac{1}{2}(2) \\
& \therefore \quad[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}]=3-4-1=-2 \\
& \therefore \left\lvert\, \frac{1}{6}\left[\begin{array} { l l l } 
{ \mathrm { x } } & { \vec { y } } & { \vec { \mathrm { r } } ] }
\end{array} \left|=\left|-\frac{1}{3}\right|=|\mathrm{c}|\right.\right.\right.
\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{x}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{y}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ are the coterminus edges of a tetrahedron whose volume is $|\mathrm{c}|$.
